items.front: What common military/civil mode is designated for ATC use?
items.back: Mode 3/A.

items.front: Which aircraft should receive beacon code assignments?
items.back: Only aircraft equipped with ADS-B and/or a transponder.

items.front: Are ADS-B-equipped aircraft still required to have an operable transponder?
items.back: Yes.

items.front: What beacon codes should normally be issued unless another rule, directive, or LOA says otherwise?
items.back: Computer-assigned beacon codes.

items.front: What code source is used for aircraft that will remain within the terminal facility's delegated airspace?
items.back: A code from the subset allocated to that terminal facility.

items.front: What code source is normally used for terminal aircraft that will enter an adjacent facility's delegated airspace?
items.back: A beacon code assigned by the ARTCC computer.

items.front: Why is an ARTCC computer-assigned code used for aircraft entering adjacent delegated airspace?
items.back: It gives the adjacent facility advance information and supports auto-acquisition before handoff.

items.front: When is Code 4000 assigned?
items.back: When the flight involves frequent or rapid altitude changes in more than one stratum or another category not compatible with a discrete code assignment.

items.front: When may you normally request a change from the transferring facility's beacon code?
items.back: After the aircraft is within your area of responsibility, unless a directive, LOA, or handoff coordination says otherwise.

items.front: What does this paragraph specifically regulate?
items.back: Requests to change the code the aircraft was squawking in the transferring facility's area.

items.front: What can authorize an earlier beacon code change before the aircraft enters your area?
items.back: A directive, a letter of agreement, or coordination at the time of handoff.

items.front: Does accepting a handoff by itself authorize an immediate code change while the aircraft is still outside your area?
items.back: No.

items.front: If no exception applies and the aircraft is still outside your area, what should you do about its code?
items.back: Leave it on the transferring facility's code until it is within your area of responsibility.

items.front: When is Code 7700 assigned?
items.back: When the pilot declares an emergency and the aircraft is not radar identified.

items.front: What phraseology is used to assign 7700 to a non-radar-identified emergency aircraft?
items.back: Squawk MAYDAY on 7700.

items.front: What does ERAM display instead of 7700?
items.back: EMRG.

items.front: What do STARS and MEARTS display instead of 7700?
items.back: EM.

items.front: After radio and radar contact are established, when may ATC request a change from 7700 to a discrete code?
items.back: When feasible, for aircraft other than single-piloted helicopters and single-piloted turbojet aircraft, considering pilot concurrence, the emergency, and flight conditions.

items.front: Which aircraft may be unable to change transponder settings during an emergency?
items.back: Single-piloted helicopters and single-piloted turbojet aircraft.

items.front: What must a terminal facility do for a Mode C equipped VFR emergency aircraft that no longer requires 7700?
items.back: Assign a beacon code that permits terminal MSAW alarm processing.

items.front: What must an en route controller do for a Mode C equipped VFR emergency aircraft that no longer requires 7700?
items.back: Make the appropriate keyboard entry to ensure en route MSAW alarm processing.

items.front: What does Code 7600 indicate for controller handling?
items.back: A communications-failure event.

items.front: What must you do when you observe Code 7600?
items.back: Apply communications-failure procedures.

items.front: What can be expected from an aircraft that loses two-way radio communications capability?
items.back: It can be expected to squawk 7600.

items.front: What does ERAM display instead of 7600?
items.back: RDOF.

items.front: What do STARS and MEARTS display instead of 7600?
items.back: RF.

items.front: What does Code 7500 indicate for controller handling?
items.back: A hijack or unlawful-interference event.

items.front: What must you do when you observe Code 7500?
items.back: Apply hijacked-aircraft procedures.

items.front: What does ERAM display instead of 7500?
items.back: HIJK.

items.front: What do STARS and MEARTS display instead of 7500?
items.back: HJ.

items.front: Does Code 7500 get handled the same way as a 7600 communications-failure display?
items.back: No.

items.front: What does Code 7400 indicate when transmitted by a UAS?
items.back: The control link between the aircraft and the pilot is lost.

items.front: When a UAS displays 7400, what should you determine first?
items.back: The lost link procedure outlined in the Special Airworthiness Certificate or the Certificate of Waiver or Authorization.

items.front: What coordination action may be required after a 7400 display?
items.back: Coordinate as required to allow the UAS to execute the lost link procedure.

items.front: When feasible, who should be advised after a UAS lost-link event is observed?
items.back: The OS/CIC.

items.front: What minimum items should be available in the lost link procedure?
items.back: Lost link route of flight, orbit points, altitudes, communications procedures, and preplanned termination points if recovery is infeasible.

items.front: Do all UAS airframes squawk 7400 during lost link?
items.back: No. Some may continue to squawk 7600, and the same lost-link procedures still apply.

items.front: If the UAS deviates from the programmed lost link procedure or another anomaly develops, how should the event be treated?
items.back: As an abnormal or emergency situation rather than routine lost-link execution alone.
